semantic_description: The fundamental knitting stitch. Inserts the needle through the front of a loop from left to right and pulls new yarn through. Produces a smooth V-shaped stitch on the right side and a bump on the wrong side. The building block of stockinette fabric.

ambiguity_notes: In some stitch dictionaries and older British patterns, 'K' means 'work in stockinette' (knit on RS, purl on WS), not strictly 'knit'. Context determines whether K means the specific action or the pattern stitch.

rs: Insert right needle into front of next stitch on left needle, from left to right
Wrap working yarn counterclockwise around right needle
Pull new loop through the stitch
Slide old stitch off left needle
ws: Produces a purl bump on the RS. The physical action is the same as on RS.

semantic_description: The reverse of a knit stitch. Inserts the needle through the front of the loop from right to left and pulls yarn through from back to front. Produces a bump on the facing side and a smooth V on the reverse. Combined with knit to create ribbing, seed stitch, and textured patterns.

rs: With yarn in front, insert right needle into front of next stitch from right to left
Wrap working yarn counterclockwise around right needle
Pull new loop through the stitch toward the back
Slide old stitch off left needle

semantic_description: A knit stitch worked through the back loop instead of the front, which twists the stitch. The resulting stitch sits twisted on the needle with legs crossed, creating a tighter, more defined stitch with a slightly different texture. Used decoratively in twisted rib and as a structural element in some lace patterns.

written_forms: knit the next stitch through the back loop
knit into the back of the next stitch
ambiguity_notes: 'tw' is used by some publishers (especially UK/German) to mean twisted knit, but can collide with 'twist' in cable contexts. 'KB' used by some older US patterns for knit through back loop.
'tbl' alone is ambiguous — some patterns use it for both ktbl and ptbl. Always check context to determine whether it modifies a knit or purl.
In combination-style knitting (Eastern European, some self-taught), stitches sit on the needle differently. What constitutes 'back loop' depends on the knitter's mount style.

rs: Insert right needle into the back leg of the next stitch (behind the left needle, from right to left)
Wrap working yarn counterclockwise around right needle
Pull new loop through
Slide old stitch off left needle

semantic_description: A purl stitch worked through the back loop, producing a twisted stitch on the reverse. The WS equivalent of knit-tbl. Creates the same twisted stitch appearance as ktbl when viewed from the right side.

written_forms: purl the next stitch through the back loop
purl into the back of the next stitch
ambiguity_notes: 'PB' can mean purl back loop or place bead depending on publisher. Always verify with legend.

rs: With yarn in front, insert right needle into the back leg of the next stitch from left to right (behind the work)
Wrap working yarn counterclockwise around right needle
Pull new loop through toward the back
Slide old stitch off left needle

semantic_description: Creates a new stitch by wrapping the yarn over the needle without consuming an existing stitch. Produces a decorative eyelet hole in the fabric. The most common increase in lace knitting, almost always paired with a corresponding decrease to maintain stitch count. The hole is the feature, not a defect.

written_forms: bring yarn to front, then over needle
wrap yarn over the right needle
ambiguity_notes: 'yfwd' and 'yf' are UK/Australian terms for yarn forward (equivalent to US yarn over between two knit stitches). 'yon' (yarn over needle) and 'yrn' (yarn round needle) are UK variants covering different stitch contexts. 'o' is used in some continental/chart-only traditions. 'm' is rare and can collide with marker instructions.

'yon' (yarn over needle) and 'yrn' (yarn round needle) are British terms that technically describe different yarn movements depending on what precedes them (knit vs purl), but all produce a yarn over.

'yf' and 'yfwd' (yarn forward) in British patterns sometimes means just bring yarn to front (for a slip stitch) rather than a full yarn over. Context is critical — if it precedes a knit stitch, it is a yarn over; if it precedes a slip, it is a yarn position change.
In patterns between two purl stitches, 'yrn' wraps the yarn completely around the needle to return to purl position. The result is still one new stitch.

rs: Bring working yarn to front of work (between needles)
Lay yarn over the right needle from front to back
Continue to next stitch — the wrap sits on the needle as a new stitch
ws: Bring working yarn to back of work
Wrap yarn over right needle from back to front

semantic_description: Wraps the yarn around the needle twice, creating two new stitches and a larger decorative hole. On the following row, the two wraps are typically worked as knit-one-purl-one (or purl-one-knit-one) to secure both loops. Creates a larger eyelet than a single yarn over. Common in lace and drop-stitch patterns.

ambiguity_notes: Some patterns chart a double yarn over as a single cell and expect the knitter to know it produces 2 stitches. Others show it as 2 cells. Check stitch count to determine which convention the pattern uses.

rs: Wrap yarn over right needle once (front to back)
Wrap yarn over right needle a second time (front to back)
Two loops now sit on the right needle

semantic_description: A yarn over that is deliberately worked twisted on the following row (through the back loop) to close the eyelet hole. Produces a new stitch like a standard yarn over but with a tighter, less visible hole. Used when the increase is structural rather than decorative.

written_forms: yarn over, then on next row work through back loop
ambiguity_notes: No universally standard abbreviation. 'yo-tbl' is the most descriptive and unambiguous. Some Haapsalu/Estonian lace charts use a specific symbol with no standard text abbreviation.

rs: Work a standard yarn over
On the following row, work this stitch through the back loop to twist it closed

semantic_description: Knits one stitch while wrapping the yarn around the needle twice instead of once. On the following row, the extra wrap is dropped, creating an elongated stitch. The stitch count does not change — the extra wrap is temporary. Used for drop-stitch patterns and elongated stitch textures.

written_forms: knit the next stitch, wrapping yarn around needle twice
knit 1 wrapping yarn twice around needle
ambiguity_notes: 'k1 wt' (knit 1 wrapping twice) can be confused with 'knit 1 with twist' in some older patterns. No CYC standard — use descriptive form in legends.

rs: Insert right needle into next stitch knitwise
Wrap yarn around right needle twice (instead of once)
Pull both wraps through the stitch
Slide old stitch off — two loops on right needle for this stitch
On the following row, drop the extra wrap to elongate the stitch

semantic_description: A pattern stitch, not a single operation — knit every row in flat knitting, or alternate knit and purl rounds in circular knitting. Produces a reversible fabric with horizontal ridges. Included here because charts sometimes use a single symbol to represent 'work in garter stitch' at a given position, meaning knit on RS and knit on WS (unlike stockinette where RS=knit, WS=purl).

ambiguity_notes: 'g st' is the CYC-style abbreviation. In chart cells garter is typically shown as a dash or knit symbol on RS with a note; rarely abbreviated in chart legends per se.

rs: Knit the stitch
ws: Knit the stitch (same action as RS — this is what makes it garter)

semantic_description: A yarn-over wrapped in the opposite direction from the standard yo. Passes the yarn over the right needle from back to front (rather than front to back), producing a twisted eyelet when knit on the next row. CYC-standard abbreviation 'byo'. Used to create a closed/tight eyelet or to balance stitch mounts after certain decreases.

written_forms: wrap yarn over the right needle from back to front
ambiguity_notes: CYC-standard abbreviation. Produces a twisted eyelet if knit normally on the following row.

rs: Bring yarn to the front of work
Pass yarn over the right needle from back to front, ending with yarn in back
